coredata.dc:description: Amides, essential structural units to construct aliphatic polyamides, peptides and proteins, have been lately demonstrated with Non-traditional intrinsic luminescence (NTIL). However, diversified compositions, structures and environments in current research render aliphatic polyamides with significant divergences in NTILs and obfuscate the fluorescent nature. Herein, we construct a library of aliphatic polyamides by thiolactone chemistry. This versatile approach equips the resultant polyamides with tunable amide category, amide distribution, and chemical environment, facilitating to uncover the structure-NTILs relationship of aliphatic fluorescent polyamides. Unexpectedly, aliphatic polyamides possess dual Polymerization-induced emissions (PIEs) due to different self or non-self hydrogen bonding interactions, which result in distinct fluorescence quenching effect upon various metal ions. Additionally, in-situ generated sulfhydryl can simplify the hetero-atom doping strategy and regulate the PIEs of aliphatic polyamides by modulating the intermolecular forces among the system or with the environment. Furthermore, the introduction of a piperazine unit provides the resultant fluorescent polyamide with lysosome-specific anchoring, which lights up the organelle. These findings not only elucidate the origin of divergent fluorescence in aliphatic polyamides, but also afford fundamental basis to develop new kinds of aliphatic polymers with controllable fluorescence and functionality.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of aliphatic fluorescent PAs from thiolactone chemistry and photophysical characterizations. (A) Monomer synthesis of CSAMA of thiolactones (M1-M6), and synthesis of corresponding PAs (P1-P6). (B) Absorbance and (C) fluorescence spectra of M1-M6 in pristine THF, 10 mg mL−1. Absorbance (D), fluorescence (E), photographs (F) of M1-M6 in THF and P1-P6 in DMF under 365 nm UV light at room temperature, and Φ (G) of M1-M6 in THF and P1-P6 in DMF, 10 mg mL−1. (H) A CIE-1931 chromaticity diagram and the positions of P1-P6 (marked by dots). (I) Φ of P1-P6 in various solvents (acetone, CHCl3, THF, DMF, methanol and H2O).

Dual emissions of P1. (A) Absorbance. (B) Relationship of absorbance at ∼ 320 nm with varying concentrations (0.5, 2, 4, 8, 16, and 32 mg mL−1). (C, D) E m375 and E m440 of P1, respectively. (E) Relationship of E m375 and E m440 intensities toward varying concentrations, respectively. (F) Excitation spectra of P1 aqueous solution under different λ em, 16 mg mL−1. (G) Photographs of P1 aqueous solutions with different concentrations and bulk P1 (letters “JSINM”) under 302 nm and 365 nm UV light at room temperature. (H) < τ > values of P1 in H2O and in bulk. (I) Fluorescence photostability of P1 aqueous solution, 4 mg mL−1.

PIE of P1. Dynamic monitoring of M1 polymerization: (A) UV–vis absorbance spectra. (B) Emission spectra. (C) FI at 375 nm and 440 nm, respectively. (D) Photographs and Φ values of P1 in H2O with different polymerization periods under 302 nm and 365 nm UV light at room temperature, and Tyndall effect (captured in dark) of P1 aqueous solution was also recorded. (E) Calculated distances between the carbonyl group and its neighboring amine group or carbonyl group for a different number of repeating units, and corresponding bond angles of amide groups were calculated as well. Absorbance and fluorescence of P1 (F) and P8 (G) in solution and in bulk (P1: H2O; P8: DMF). (H) Φ values of P1 and P8 in solution and in bulk (P1: H2O; P8: DMF). (I) < τ > of P8. (J) Emission of P8 in mixed solvents of DMF and H2O with varied volume ratios, which were excited at 300 nm and 375 nm, respectively, 0.5 mg mL−1.

Possible emission centers in aliphatic PAs. (A) Amide I/II regions FTIR spectra of different PAs (P1, P6 and P8). (B) FI of P1 at different pH values at 375 nm and 440 nm, respectively. (C) FI of P1 in H2O and D2O at 375 nm and 440 nm, respectively. (D) Absorbance and (E) emission spectra of P1 aqueous solution (5 mg mL−1) treated with H2O2 (3 eq) and APS (3 eq). Fluorescence spectra of P8 powder (F) and tablet (G) with varied λ ex or λ em. Insets are photographs of P8 powder and tablet taken under 365 nm UV light. (H) < τ > values of P8 powder and tablet. (I) Cryo-induced fluorescence enhancement of P1 and P8 in solution (P1: H2O; P8: DMF) and in bulk from 298 K to 77 K.

Fabrication of aliphatic fluorescent PAs with controllable fluorescence. (A) Chemical structures of P9-P13. (B) Photographs and Φ values of P9-P13 in H2O under 365 nm UV light at 298 K, and Tyndall effect in H2O was also recorded. (C-F) Excitation and emission spectra of P9-P12 aqueous solutions, 10 mg mL−1. (G) Absorbance spectra. (H) Fluorescence lifetime. (I) Cryo-induced FI enhancement from 298 K to 77 K, and Φ values of P9-P12 in H2O and in bulk were recorded. (J) Amide I/II regions FTIR spectra.

Distinct PIE quenching effect of P1 by metal ions. Relative fluorescence intensity (A), photographs (B), and absorbance spectra (C) of P1 (1 mM) aqueous solution (E x300, E x375) upon incubation of 10 mM metal ions (Li+, K+, Cu+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Zr4+) for 1 h. Fluorescence spectra (D, E) and Log(F0/F) (F) of P1 aqueous solution incubation of Ag+ at various concentrations (0, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 mM). G) Proposed PIE reduction mechanism of P1 aqueous solution upon incubation with metal ions.

P4 nanoprobe for lysosome imaging. (A) Chemical structure of P4. (B, C) Diameter and surface potential of P4 nanoparticle. (D) CCK-8 assay. (E-H) CLSM colocalization experiment and analysis (lysosome and mitochondria) of P4 nanoprobe in HepG2 cell. Scale bar, 25 μm.

head.ce:title: Uncovering divergent fluorescence of aliphatic polyamides: Synthesis, dual polymerization-induced emissions, and organelle-specific imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amides, essential structural units to construct aliphatic polyamides, peptides and proteins, have been lately demonstrated with Non-traditional intrinsic luminescence (NTIL). However, diversified compositions, structures and environments in current research render aliphatic polyamides with significant divergences in NTILs and obfuscate the fluorescent nature. Herein, we construct a library of aliphatic polyamides by thiolactone chemistry. This versatile approach equips the resultant polyamides with tunable amide category, amide distribution, and chemical environment, facilitating to uncover the structure-NTILs relationship of aliphatic fluorescent polyamides. Unexpectedly, aliphatic polyamides possess dual Polymerization-induced emissions (PIEs) due to different self or non-self hydrogen bonding interactions, which result in distinct fluorescence quenching effect upon various metal ions. Additionally, in-situ generated sulfhydryl can simplify the hetero-atom doping strategy and regulate the PIEs of aliphatic polyamides by modulating the intermolecular forces among the system or with the environment. Furthermore, the introduction of a piperazine unit provides the resultant fluorescent polyamide with lysosome-specific anchoring, which lights up the organelle. These findings not only elucidate the origin of divergent fluorescence in aliphatic polyamides, but also afford fundamental basis to develop new kinds of aliphatic polymers with controllable fluorescence and functionality.

ce:section.ce:para: The discovery of intrinsic fluorescence in nonaromatic materials (e.g., protein, peptide, rice, etc.) has remarkably advanced current fluorescence paradigm, because it not only extends the traditional principles and establishes new theories [1–5], but also provides clues for exploiting the photochemical reaction and physical process in various scientific problems. Meanwhile, because of improved biocompatibility, biodegradability and aqueous solubility, aliphatic fluorescent polymers have shown more privileges in biomedical and biological fields over existing fluorescent pigments containing aromatic moieties [5–7]. As the same primary components in many polymers, amides were often used as fluorescence quenchers due to the forbidden transition and the electron transfer [8], so the fluorescence from aliphatic polyamides (PAs) is limited and controversial. In the past decade, an amide has been gradually recognized a luminogen with Non-traditional intrinsic luminescence (NTIL), and several fluorescent aliphatic PAs have been fabricated [4]. Yet, the details of aliphatic amides in fluorescence contribution have not been fully understood.

Arranging amides in varied sequence orders and configurations could differentially constitute aliphatic peptides, proteins and PAs. Despite of comparable compositions, delicate structures (category and distribution of amides) and chemical environment (e.g., amphiphilicity and hetero atom doping of PAs) of amides in different PAs can deviate significantly, so large spans (>100 nm) in both fluorescence excitation maximum (E x) and emission maximum (E m) of aliphatic PAs are frequently observed [4]. Shukla, Mercato and Sharpe successively reported the fluorescence from aliphatic peptides/proteins in solution and/or in bulk (E x: 340–405 nm, E m: 425–500 nm), which was ascribed to the electron delocalization through intramolecular/intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions[9–11]. Tang’s group demonstrated two synthetic aliphatic PAs, namely poly(N-isopropylacrylamide and poly(N-tert-butylacrylamide), also had intrinsic fluorescence due to strong amide-amide hydrogen bonding interactions (E x ∼ 340 nm, E m ∼ 370 nm) [12]. Recently, Singha and co-workers reported that branched aliphatic terpolymers comprising secondary/tertiary amides also possessed intrinsic fluorescence (E x ∼ 300 nm, E m ∼ 410 nm), and Aggregation induced enhanced emission (AIEE) was observed [13]. This phenomenon of fluorescence divergence is often regarded as the presence of multiple fluorescence centers, but no more profound evidence is available from the research endeavors to date. Besides, we notice two important issues in these researches, which could possibly differentiate the resultant fluorescence of aliphatic PAs. First, amide is not the only possible luminogen through PAs, namely the fluorescence interference is not excluded from the research object. Second, the amide category in PAs is mixed and the amide distribution is uncertain, rendering the fluorescence-structure relationship more complicated. Thus, it is of impendence to design and synthesize aliphatic fluorescent PAs with tunable structures, so as to further distinguish their diversified NTILs.

In this contribution, we utilize thiolactone building blocks to construct various aliphatic PAs. By thiolactone chemistry, the category and distribution of amides can be regulated via altering ring-opening reagents, terminal groups of thiolactones, and polymerization methods. Moreover, the in-situ formed sulfhydryl not only can act as a doped hetero atom, but also can be used to tune the flexibility and amphiphilicity of PAs via Thiol-X chemistry [14]. Based on these features, a library of aliphatic PAs with diverse amide units and substructures were prepared, and dual Polymerization-induced emission (PIE) centers from aliphatic fluorescent PAs are demonstrated for the first time. Specifically, aliphatic PAs containing different amide units (primary amide, secondary amide and tertiary amide) present distinct PIEs due to discrepant local chemical environment and varying self or non-self-intermolecular forces, which lead to different fluorescence quenching effect upon metal ions. In addition, the PIE properties can be readily adjusted by altering intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions throughout PAs. Interestingly, by employing a piperazine-containing ring-opening reagent, the resultant PA presents lysosome-specific anchoring and selectively lights up the organelle. This study may not only afford the clues between the structures and NTILs of aliphatic PAs, but also will shed new light on PIEs of aliphatic polymers for precision imaging.

D,L-Homocysteine thiolactone hydrochloride (Sigma-Aldrich, ≥99%), methacryloyl chloride (Sigma-Aldrich, 97%), acryloyl chloride (TCI, 98%), acetyl chloride (TCI, >98%), methyl chloroformate (Aldrich, 99%), pivaloyl chloride (TCI, >98%), 4-nitrobenzoyl chloride (TCI, >98%), α-bromoisobutyryl bromide (Sigma-Aldrich, 98%), benzyl chloroformate (TCI, >96%), methyl 3-chloro-3-oxopropanoate (TCI, >97%), pyrrolidine (TCI, >98%), piperidine (Sigma-Aldrich, ≥99%), azepane (Alfa Aesar, >98%), piperazine (TCI, >98%), 1-methylpiperazine (TCI, >98%), 1-methylpiperidine (TCI, >99%), morpholine (TCI, >99%), pyrrole (TCI, >99%), imidazole (TCI, >99%), indoline (Acros, 98%), n-butylamine (TCI, >99%), ethanolamine (Sigma-Aldrich, ≥98%), allylamine (Xiya reagent, 98%), 2,2-dimethoxy-2-phenylacetophenone (DMPA, Aladdin, 99%), diethylamine (Sigma-Aldrich, ≥99.5%), sodium azide (Sangon Biotech, >99%), 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP, Acros, 99%), triphosgene (Aladdin, 99%), poly(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate (PEG500, average M n ∼ 500, Aldrich), t-butyl methacrylate (TCI, >98%), tris-(2-carboxyethyl)-phosphine (TCEP, Acros, 98%), quinine sulfate (Aladdin, 98.6%), fluorescein 5-isothiocyanate (FITC, Alfa-Aesar, >95%), 2-(4-Amidinophenyl)-6-indolecarbamidine dihydrochloride (DAPI, Beyotime), Lyso-Tracker Red (Beyotime), Mito-Tracker Red (Beyotime), ammonium peroxydisulfate (Alfa Aesar, >98%), N-isopropylmethacrylamide (Ark Pharm, 97%), N,N-dimethacrylamide (TCI, >95%), methacrylamide (Acros, 98%, extra pure). 2,2′-Azodiisobutyronitrile (AIBN, TCI, >98%) was recrystallized twice from ethanol. Methyl sulfoxide (Acros, 99.7%, extra dry), N, N-dimethylformamide (Acros, 99.8%, extra dry), methanol (TCI, HPLC grade, >99.8%), tetrahydrofuran (>99.8%, anhydrous, Alfa Aesar), acetone (Acros, 99.8%, extra dry), triethylamine (Acros, extra pure, 99.7%), deuterium oxide (D2O, Aldrich, 99.9 atom % D). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt% solution in water), metal salts ((Li+, K+, Cu+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Zr4+) and other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (SCRC) and used as received.

1H Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and 13C NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker Avance 400 spectrometer (resonance frequency of 400 MHz for 1H and 100 MHz for 13C). High resolution mass spectrum (HRMS) analysis was performed using an LTQ Orbitrap XL (Thermo Scientific) Mass spectrometer (MS) or using a Xevo C2-S quadrupole time-of-flight (Q-TOF) MS (Waters, Milford, Massachusetts, USA). UV was recorded on a UV-2601 UV-vis spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU). Fluorescence and photostability test were measured on a LS55 Fluorescence Spectrometer (PerkinElmer). Fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield were recorded on a FLS980 Fluorescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments). The molecular weights and Polydispersities index (PDI) of polymers were determined by a Waters Breeze2 Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) with a 1500 injection pump, a 2489 UV/Vis detector and a 2414 IR detector. DMF was utilized as an eluent with a flow rate of 1 mL min−1 at 30 °C and polystyrenes (Polymer Laboratories, Inc., MA) were used for calibration. IR spectroscopy was conducted using KBr pellets/films on a TENSOR 27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker). The pH values of polymer solutions were controlled via a PP-20-P11 pH meter (Sartorius). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) analysis was performed on a JEM-2100 (JEOL Ltd.) analytical electron microscope at 200 kV. Atomic force microscope (AFM) was recorded on a Bruker Dimension Icon with ScanAsyst. The determinations of hydrodynamic diameters and surface potentials of PA aqueous solutions were performed by Dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 with a He–Ne laser. Cell imaging was recorded on a TCS SP8 confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica, Germany). All data were analyzed and processed using Origin 2019b software unless otherwise stated.

The N-substituted thiolactone library was synthesized from the starting materials of D,L-homocysteine thiolactone and various acyl chlorides or chloroformates. Typically, D,L-homocysteine thiolactone hydrochloride (0.02 mmol) was mixed with 2.2 equivalent triethylamine in 50 mL dichloromethane (DCM) to form a suspension. An equivalent of acyl chloride or chloroformate was then added dropwise in an ice bath, and the mixture was stirred overnight at room temperature. Afterwards, the reaction mixture was diluted with DCM, filtered, washed with brine, and extracted with DCM twice. The organic layer was dried with anhydrous Na2SO4, vacuum concentrated, and purified by silica gel column chromatography to obtain the product. The 1H, 13C NMR data were given in supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aminolysis of various N-substituted thiolactones by cyclic secondary amines
ce:section.ce:para: All aminolysis of N-substituted thiolactones were performed by equal feed ratios of a thiolactone and a cyclic secondary amine. Typically, the aminolysis reaction was performed in deoxidized chloroform and reacted at 30 °C overnight. Then, the product was subjected to 1H, 13C NMR and HRMS analysis without further purification. The evaluation of solvents, substitutes of thiolactones, kinds of amines (cyclic secondary amines, cyclic tertiary amines, primary amines, and linear secondary amines) was performed with a similar synthetic procedure. The 1H, 13C NMR and HRMS data were given in supplementary materials.

Methacrylamide monomers were prepared by one-pot synthesis of ring-opening reaction of thiolactones and thiol-methacrylate Michael addition reaction. Generally, N-methacrylhomocysteine thiolactone (N-MAHCT, 37.1 mg, 0.2 mmol) and PEG500 (100.2 mg, 0.2 mmol) was dissolved in 1 mL anhydrous THF/DMSO (v/v = 1/1) and purged with argon for 10 min. Then, an equivalent of secondary cyclic amine was added into the mixture and was stirred at 30 °C overnight under dark. All methacrylamide monomers were purified by silica gel column chromatography (hexane : ethyl acetate = 1:1, v/v) to remove unreacted reagents. Methacrylamide monomers that derived from the aminolysis by primary amines were prepared in a similar manner. The 1H, 13C NMR and HRMS data of M1-M8 were provided in supplementary materials.

The corresponding linear polyamides from methacrylamide monomers were basically prepared via free radical polymerizations (P1-P8). Taking P1 as an example, 1 mL M1 (100.1 mg) THF solution containing 0.5 wt% AIBN was added into a glass tube and subjected to freeze–thaw cycle twice to exclude air. Then, the tube was sealed under vacuum and was subsequently placed in a preheated oil bath at 65 °C. Upon predetermined periods, the resultant mixture was purified by precipitating into mixed solvents of ether/hexane (1/1, v/v) and was dried under vacuum for 3 h to generate viscous pale or slight yellow products. Yield: 67.3%.

Alternatively, linear polyamides can be prepared from click chemistry or Michael addition reaction (P9, P10). Thiol was generated from the aminolysis of a thiolactone monomer, while the alkene group can be introduced via either a ring-opening agent or an alkene-terminated thiolactone monomer. Taking P9 as an example, N-acetylhomocysteine thiolactone (N-ACHCT, 95.4 mg, 0.6 mmol) and allylamine (37.6 mg, 0.66 mmol) was dissolved in 0.6 mL dioxane, and stirred at room temperature overnight. Then, TCEP (10 mM) was added into the mixture and stirred 1 h to reduce possible disulfide bonds. Afterwards, DMPA (1 wt%) was added as a photoinitiator, and the reaction mixture was exposed to UV irradiation for 30 min. The resulting mixture was purified by precipitating into ether and was dried under vacuum for 3 h to yield a viscous solid. Yield: 78.4%. P14 was prepared with a similar procedure as P10 in DMSO, except without adding PEG500 to consume sulfhydryls.

For P11, P12 and P13, conventional radical polymerization of corresponding commercial monomers of N-isopropylmethacrylamide, N,N-dimethylacrylamide and methacrylamide with purification in diethyl ether generated white powders. Yields: 85.4% (P11), 92.7% (P12), 96.3% (P13).

HepG2 cells (1 × 104 cells per mL medium) were seeded and cultured on in Lab-Tek chambered coverglass wells. After 24 h, 10 μM of P4 was added and incubated for 60 min. Then, the cells were washed with fresh PBS for three times. For colocalization experiment, the HepG2 cells were co-stained with P4 for 45 min and subsequently with Lyso-Tracker Red or Mito-Tracker Red (100 nM) for 10 min. Free organelle staining agents were discarded by PBS washing. Finally, the cell nuclei were labeled with DAPI. Excitation and emission filters for each agent were as follows: for DAPI: excitation filter = 405 nm, emission filter = 420–470 nm; for P4, excitation filter = 488 nm, emission filter = 490–570 nm; for Lyso-Tracker Red, excitation filter = 545 nm, and emission filter = 630–700 nm; for Mito-Tracker Red, excitation filter = 545 nm, and emission filter = 580–700 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aminolysis of thiolactones by secondary amines
ce:section.ce:para: During the past decade, thiolactone chemistry has shown promise in tailored synthesis and dual-modification of polymers [14]. Typically, the aminolysis of a thiolactone occurs readily with a primary amine, generating a linear secondary amide with a pendent sulfhydryl [15]. However, this reaction hardly takes place with a linear secondary amine or a tertiary amine. Exception was reported when a cyclic secondary amine was used with or without a catalytic Lewis acid, which led to the formation of a linear tertiary amide [16–18]. Same as previous works, in the control aminolysis of N-MAHCT with pyrrolidine at an equivalent ratio, complete ring-opening of the thiolactone and quantitative conversion into a tertiary amide were verified by 1H, 13C NMR and HRMS (Fig. S10). This aminolysis reaction of N-MAHCT was feasible with other secondary cyclic amines such as piperidine, azepane, piperazine, 1-methylpiperazine, morpholine and indoline (Scheme S1, Figs. S11–S15), and the aminolysis efficiency declined with decreasing pKa values of amines. It is known that secondary amines with more carbon atoms are more nucleohpilic than the smaller ones. However, the nucleophilicity cannot exactly reflect its reactivity because of hindrance effects [19]. Pyrrole and imidazole also have secondary amine units, but their low nucleophilicities could not ring-open N-MAHCT, neither could cyclic tertiary amines (e.g., 1-methylpiperidine) or linear secondary amines (e.g., diethylamine) (Figs. S16–S19). Moreover, this Cyclic secondary amine-mediated aminolysis (CSAMA) was efficient in various solvents (Table S1, Figs. S20–S23) and ran smooth with varying N-substituted homocysteine thiolactones (Scheme S2, Figs. S1–S9, S24–S30). Taken together, CSAMA of thiolactones is a versatile reaction and expands the amine category toward the conventional aminolysis of γ-thiolactones.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NTILs of aliphatic PAs from thiolactone chemistry
ce:section.ce:para: CSAMA of thiolactones enriches structures of resultant amides in PAs. For example, the aminolysis of a N-MAHCT by pyrrolidine, piperidine, azepane, piperazine, morpholine with orthogonal PEGylation generated a serial of methacrylamide monomers (M1-M5) (Fig. 1 A, Figs. S31–S35, Table S3), which possessed both a secondary amide and a tertiary amide. PEGylation was employed to ensure complete consumption of in-situ formed thiols and exclude potential thiol-thiol oxidation. These monomers presented typical absorbance and weak fluorescence at ∼ 325 nm and ∼ 355 nm, respectively (Fig. 1B, 1C). Through conventional radical polymerization (Figs. S37–S41), the absorbance of formed PAs (P1-P5) still resided at ∼ 325 nm, but their E m wavelengths red-shifted in DMF with different degrees (360–468 nm, Fig. 1C, 1D). In addition, P1-P5 achieved much higher quantum yields (Φ) than those of monomers (Fig. 1G). The corresponding CIE coordinates of P1-P5 all located in regions for blue LED (Fig. 1H). Although with comparable molecular weights, differences in substructures (ring size and hetero atoms in cyclic amines) resulted in varying fluorescence enhancement. Amongst, P1 showed the highest Φ of 27.1% in DMF. Due to the solvent and environmental effect [20], P1-P5 presented different photophysical parameters in various solvents and the results were summarized in Fig. 1I and Table S2. To survey NTILs from these aliphatic PAs, we prepared M6 and M7 as a control (Fig. 1A, Figs. S36, S49, Table S3), in which N-MAHCT was ring-opened by a primary amine (n-butylamine and ethanolamine, respectively), thus resultant P6 and P7 would merely have secondary amides (Fig. 1A, Figs. S42, S51), but without tertiary amides.

To get more information of photophysical properties from PAs, we further determined their emissions with varying excitation wavelengths (λ ex). Fig. S44 reveals that P1-P6 all demonstrated dual distributions in emission spectra, one emission was around ∼ 375 nm (E m375) and the other was above 420 nm (>E m420). The former emission was almost λ ex-independent, while the latter was λ ex-dependent [21,22]. The coexistence of λ ex-dependent and λ ex-independent NTILs was previously observed in graphene quantum dots, which originated from the defect state and the size effect, respectively [23]. More precisely, we analyzed shifted wavelengths of the two emissions upon respective designated excitation regions ([E x255, E x300] for E m375, and [E x345, E x390] for > E m420). Interestingly, the wavelengths of E m375 for P1-P3 were nearly constant upon different λ ex, but additional oxygen or nitrogen atoms in P4 and P5 caused slight blue shifts in emission wavelengths (λ em) (Fig. S44). Due to different amides within PAs, P6 presented a larger blue shift (Δλ em > 15 nm) at E m375 upon varying λ ex, and their Φ values in water were lower than that of P1 (Fig. S45). On the other hand, >E m420 of all PAs exhibited red-shifted λ em upon increasing λ ex ([E x345, E x390]). Amongst, P1 presented the smallest red-shifted λ em of 8 nm, without obvious fluorescence sacrifice and achieving the highest Φ. These results indicate that there are more than one NTILs in aliphatic PAs.

Concentration is a crucial parameter to adjust NTILs and to identify emission centers [24–26]. Recently, Yuan’s group demonstrated high concentration could lead to Clustering-triggered emission (CTE) of proteins and nonaromatic oxygen clusters [6,27]. Therefore, we studied the photophysical properties of P1 with varying concentrations (0.5, 2 , 4, 8, 16, 32 mg mL−1 aqueous solutions and bulk P1) by UV/Vis and fluorescence spectroscopies (Fig. 2 ). As depicted in Fig. 2A, 2B, typical absorption of P1 aqueous solution at 320 nm was proportional to concentration (R 2 = 0.9990), and higher concentrations ensure stronger absorptions at longer wavelengths (λ > 350 nm). All P1 aqueous solutions possess two distinct emissions at ∼ 375 nm (E m375) and ∼ 440 nm (E m440) upon varying λ ex (Fig. S46), while bulk P1 only presented mono-emission at 446 nm, which possibly indicate E m375 is related with the solvent interaction. E m375 increased nonlinearly and arrived at a plateau when the concentration was over 16 mg mL−1, while E m440 exhibited a linear increase (R 2 = 0.9992) even when the concentration was high up to 32 mg mL−1 (Fig. 2C-2E). The fluorescence excitation spectra verify the presence of two separate emission centers in P1 (Fig. 2F), with maximal λ ex at ∼ 300 nm (E x300) and ∼ 375 nm (E x375), respectively.

By contrast, in the fluorescence spectra of P6 aqueous solution, only one brief excitation (E x370) was detected and E x302 was the shoulder signal, but not an individual maximal excitation (Fig. S47). Therefore, P6 has only one emission center, and the corresponding E m of P6 in H2O and in bulk were 437 nm and 429 nm, respectively. These results indicate that different amides in aliphatic PAs lead to distinct NTILs. For P1, E m440 presented a cyan emission while E m375 showed blue fluorescence in aqueous solutions with varied concentrations and in bulk, and the Φ of E m440 was always higher than that of E m375 (Fig. 2G). In general, fluorescent dyes with higher Φ values accompany with longer fluorescence lifetimes (< τ >) and can provide imaging application with a good contrast [28]. As shown in Fig. 2H, the < τ > of E m375 is 0.58/3.89 ns (τ1/τ2), while E m440 has a longer lifetime of 1.43/4.58 ns (τ1/τ2). This difference possibly ascribes from coexistence of secondary amides and tertiary amides within the structure of P1. Absent of amide-amide hydrogen bonding interactions, tertiary amides exhibit better rotatability than secondary amides, and thus contributing to decreased fluorescence and shorter < τ > [29]. Due to increasing structural congestion, P1 have longer < τ > values in bulk state than those in H2O. Additionally, both emissions were photostable upon continuous light irradiation, which were superior to fluorescein 5-isothiocyanate (FITC) and comparable to quinine sulfate (Fig. 2I). It should be noted that as-obtained dual emissions of aliphatic PAs are from distinct fluorescence centers, but not λ ex-dependent multicolor emissions from an ensemble of fluorescent states or a quantum confinement [30,31]. This kind of emissive materials not only can be excited with different λ ex, but also can prohibit the compromise in fluorescence intensity (FI) [32].

Based on these results, the NTILs of aliphatic PAs in our system essentially fit PIE. We discovered similar phenomenon in 2012 [33], and recently Wan’s group confirmed it via Barbier hyperbranching strategy [34]. Herein, we dynamically monitored the polymerization of M1 by absorbance and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 3 A, 3B). Notably, the molecular weight of P1 increased with polymerization periods (Fig. S48), with enhanced absorbance peaks at ∼ 324 nm and ∼ 351 nm. M1 was weakly fluorescent and had Φ values of 2.6% and 0.5% at λ exs of 300 and 375 nm, respectively. Upon polymerization, FI of E m375 increased continuously up to 8 h, and an enhanced Φ around 4.0% was obtained. On the other hand, FI of E m440 presented a rising tendency but decreased slightly at 8 h (Fig. 3C). This fluorescence reduction probably resulted from self-absorption and/or exciton interactions [35,36], and the final Φ of E m440 approached 9%. As the polymerization goes on, small molecules turn into oligomers and polymers with more chain entanglement, and the Tyndall effect (captured in dark) indicated possible clustering of P1 after polymerization (Fig. 3D). Density functional theory (DFT) calculation was employed to study the optimized geometry change of P1 as the number of repeating unit (n) increased. Fig. 3E reveals that the distance of neighboring carbonyls decreased from 3.49 Å to 3.27 Å as n increased from 1 to 3, and the distance between amine and neighboring carbonyl (with cyclic amine) decreased from 2.42 Å to 2.40 Å. Meanwhile, the bond angle between amine and neighboring carbonyl (with cyclic amine) decreased from 107.6° to 106.9°. All these variations of configurations demonstrated that molecular chains of P1 rotated and packed into a more compact structure after polymerization, thus leading to enhanced fluorescence.

Polymers with PIEs generally emit both in solution and in bulk [33,34]. E x300 resulted in E m of P1 with 12 nm gap in H2O and in bulk, while E x375 did not cause obvious change to E m440 (Fig. 3F). As is known, a rigid structure could decrease the flexibility of polymer chains and restrict the non-radiative decay pathways, thus an enhanced Φ is often acquired. Therefore, t-butyl methacrylate replaced PEG500 to capture free sulfhydryls, generating P8 as a rigid and hydrophobic aliphatic PA (Scheme S3, Table S3, Fig. S50, S52). Similar to P1, P8 also has two emission centers that can be excited at both 300 and 380 nm (Fig. S53). In Fig. 3G and Fig. S55, the emission from E x300 blue-shifted to E m353 and shared the same emission in DMF and in bulk, while the emission from E x380 resulted in a gap of 9 nm. Blue-shifted λ em generally hints the generation of a rigid environment. Therefore, different blue-shifted wavelengths in emission spectra demonstrate distinct molecular packing in bulk P1 and P8. For both P1 and P8, emissions from longer λ exs have higher Φ and longer < τ > values both in solution and in bulk (Fig. 3H, 3I). Different from AIE, even at a low concentration of ∼ 0.05 mM in THF (solvent), P1 was also strong fluorescent and has Φ values of 4.5% and 15.1% at E m375 and E m434, respectively. Moreover, when adding water (nonsolvent) into DMF solution of P8, no AIE effect was observed upon E x300 or E x380 (Fig. 3J, Fig. S56). This discovery is very different from previous fluorescent PAs, which usually present AIE or AIEE properties [12,13]. To exclude the possibility of the formation of polymer aggregates in the assumed good solvents, we characterized the sizes of variable P1 aqueous solutions (1 h, 2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 8 h samples, 5 mg mL−1) by DLS analysis. As shown in Fig. S57, the sizes of all samples reside below 5 nm, which is much smaller than the typical size (>50 nm for aliphatic polymers, and > 100 nm for aromatic polymers) of polymeric nanoaggregates [37,38]. Therefore, in our aliphatic PAs system, no obvious AIE characteristics were observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible NTIL origins from aliphatic PAs
ce:section.ce:para: Analyzing the structure of P1, the possible fluorescent species could be tertiary amide and secondary amide, and the fluorescence interference from PEG500 and polyPEG500 were both excluded (Fig. S58). Of note, secondary amide can form self-intermolecular hydrogen bond while tertiary amide cannot, but they can both form hydrogen bond with protic solvents. As shown in Fig. 4 A, P1, P6 and P8 have similar amide II band at 1525 cm −1 , and the coexistence of secondary and tertiary amides provided P1 and P8 with a lower amide I band at 1633 cm−1 than that of P6 (1656 cm−1). In the range of 3550–3200 cm−1, typical peak of free N − H stretch band was noticed at 3514 cm−1, while hydrogen-bonded N − H stretch band was detected at 3295, 3295 and 3303 cm−1 for P1, P6 and P8 [39], respectively. Briefly, more secondary amide units and more rigid structure endow bulk PAs with stronger intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions. As deuterium oxide (D2O) has stronger hydrogen bond interaction than H2O and has been demonstrated to stabilize the proton transfer rate between a dye and a solvent, thus D2O could enhance both FI and < τ > [40–42]. Therefore, we then recorded the emissions of P1 in D2O. As expected, E m440 in D2O is stronger than that in H2O at varying concentrations (1–32 mg mL−1), but E m375 did not show obvious difference in D2O (Fig. 4B, Fig. S59). Also, pH plays a vital role in NTILs of polymers owing to nitrogen-atom-containing groups [43–45]. E m440 presented the strongest fluorescence in neutral condition, and both acidic and basic environments slightly decreased the FI (Fig. 4C, Fig. S60). By contrast, E m375 showed intensified fluorescence in acidic condition, which is pervasive in nitrogen atom-involving polymers and is related with the protonation of nitrogen atoms [43–46]. In the structure of P1, owing to more strain than the linear secondary amide, N-protonation is favored in cyclic tertiary amide with the stabilizing contribution from the polar solvent (H2O) [47], thus can lead to potential fluorescence enhancement in acidic environment. Moreover, due to the significance of hetero atoms (N, O, S and P) in NTILs [48–51], we tested the emissions of P1 interacting with oxidants as well. As shown in Fig. 4D, 4E and S61, the FI of E m375 increased slightly with incubation of H2O2 (3 eq), and incubation with ammonium persulphate (APS, 3 eq) increased the FI of E m375 by 40%. By contrast, the FI of E m440 presented fluorescence decrease upon oxidation, and incubation of P1 aqueous solution with APS (3 eq) decreased the FI of E m440 by 63%. Addition of H2O2 and APS affords additional hydrogen bonding interaction between amides and oxidants, meanwhile weakens the intermolecular hydrogen bonding interaction among amides (Fig. S62), thus can attenuate the fluorescence of E m440. UV–Vis spectra manifest the absorption at 323 nm decreased apparently with the appearance of a new absorption above 362 nm, which indicated the different fluorescence nature of E m375 and E m440.

Dense molecular packing/aggregation could suppress nonradiative decay pathways and boost NTILs [12,27]. Since PEGylation renders P1 difficult in molding due to medium viscosity, we chose bulk P8 and pressed the powder into a tablet. Increased concentration of local intermolecular hydrogen bonds from secondary amides provided P8 tablet with an enhanced Φ of 32.3%. Albeit resembling fluorescence profiles, P8 powder/tablet have different dual E ms at 350/336 and 461/471 nm, respectively, which were also evidenced by their distinct < τ > values and excitation spectra (Fig. 4F–4H, S63). Compared to the powder, P8 tablet has a longer < τ > of E m440 due to increased structural rigidity. However, < τ > of E m375 was shortened, which frequently occurs in a fluorescence quenching process [28]. Considering both E m375 of P1 and P8 exhibited fluorescence decreasing or quenching in concentrated solutions or in bulk, we further studied the fluorescence of aliphatic PAs in solvents with varying hydrogen bonding interactions. Basically, P1 also presented dual emission centers in both DMF (E m377, E m438) and CHCl3 (E m375, E m438), and the concentration-dependent fluorescence showed a similar profile to that in H2O (Figs. S64, S65). However, the FI of E m375 in CHCl3 was much weaker than those in DMF and H2O, accompanying with the highest fluorescence intensity ratio of E m438/E m375 (Fig. S66). This solvent effect is common in NTILs and possibly results from the different interaction intensities between solvents and polymer segments, which would influence the extent of polymer aggregation [52].

Besides, cryogenic condition could suppress the molecular movement of materials and have a significant impact on fluorescence [53,54]. At 77 K, P1 aqueous solution (5 mg mL−1) and bulk P1 exhibited similar emission spectra upon varying λ ex, with maximal E m at 420 and 426 nm, respectively (Fig. S67). Notably, no emission in region of 330–380 nm was observed in aqueous solution at such concentration, which was ascribed to the lack or the restriction of the hydrogen bonding interaction through the system or with the environment. Compared to samples at 298 K, the fluorescence of P1 aqueous solution and bulk P1 at 77 K increased by 6.9 and 4.1 fold, respectively (Fig. 4I). Because of decreasing molecular vibration at a frozen state, P1 achieved a blue-shifted E m around ∼ 12–15 nm. In the case of P8, a similar fluorescence profile appeared at 77 K, but the fluorescence enhancement from 298 K to 77 K was rather limited due to its intrinsic rigid structure, leading to a much smaller enhancement space than P1 (Fig. S67). Combining these results, we speculate that E m440 stemmed only from secondary amides and was related with their intermolecular hydrogen bonding interaction, while E m375 resulted from both tertiary amides and secondary amides and was dependent on the hydrogen bonding interaction with the solvent. But in many cases, amide-amide hydrogen bonding is much stronger than amide-solvent bonding [55]. Thus, E m375 in aliphatic PAs that are contributed from secondary amides cannot always be detected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regulating the NTILs of aliphatic PAs
ce:section.ce:para: To verify our hypothesis that different amides contribute to distinct PIEs, we synthesized a serial of aliphatic PAs containing varied amides (P9-P14, Table S3, Figs. S68–S73), and their chemical structures were represented in Fig. 5 A and Scheme S3. Specifically, P9 and P10 have only secondary amides, with sulphur atom and/or PEG doping through the main chain and/or side chain of PAs. P11, P12 and P13 possess merely secondary amides, tertiary amides and primary amides, respectively, without hetero atom or PEG doping. Because all these PAs have merely one kind of amides, P9-P13 should have one emission center if they are fluorescent. Fig. 5C reveals that P9 have mono E x and E m at 385 and 487 nm, respectively. Due to presence of additional secondary amine in the backbone, hydrogen bond could form between secondary amines and carbonyl groups within PA backbone and thus possibly contributed additional fluorescence. Therefore, adjacent dual E xs at 338 and 373 nm appeared in P10, with corresponding E ms at 416 and 438 nm, separately (Fig. 5D). However, in P10 aqueous solutions with various concentrations and bulk P10, only mono E m was noticed upon varying λ ex (Fig. S76). Indeed, the structure of P10 was similar to that of poly(amido amine) and excitation-dependent fluorescence was observed. Collectively, the fluorescence contribution from amides of P10 has mono emission. This circumstance also happened in P11, with dual E x at 258 and 333 nm and mono E m around ∼ 423 nm (Fig. 5E). Typical mono E x265 and E m335 were obtained in the case of P12 as well (Fig. 5F). However, P13 was nonfluorescent in H2O but showed detectable fluorescence in CHCl3 (Fig. S74), indicating possible proton transfer between primary amides and H2O, which was also observed in aliphatic fluorescent poly(amide-imide)s[56]. Furthermore, λ ex-dependent emission, concentration-dependent emission and cryogenic experiments were thoroughly investigated to confirm emission centers of these PAs (Figs. S75–S82). Collectively, P9-P12 exhibited a comparable fluorescence profile as P1. Amongst, P9 have the highest Φ (40.6% in H2O), < τ > values (τ 1 = 2.52 ns, τ 2 = 12.54 ns in H2O) and the largest cryo-induced FI enhancement (8.8-fold in H2O) (Fig. 5B, 5H-5I). By measuring the Tyndall effect (captured in dark) and Derived count rate (DCR) via Dynamic light scattering (DLS), all aqueous solutions of PAs showed obvious clustering and P9 possessed the highest DCR (Fig. 5B). FT-IR spectroscopy was then used to explore the molecular interactions among PAs. Fig. 5J indicates only hydrogen-bonded N − H stretch band at 3279 cm−1 was noticed in P9, while additional free N − H stretch band at 3459 cm−1 existed in both P10 and P11. These results demonstrate the hydrogen bonding interaction in the backbone of PA was stronger than that in the side-chain of PA, and involved sulphur atom can possibly strengthen the hydrogen bonding interaction among PA [57,58]. This is consistent with previous research that both the doped hetero atom and the chemical structure determine the doping effect [59]. Interestingly, strong free N − H stretch band was observed at 3428 cm−1 in P13, with a weak hydrogen-bonded N − H stretch band at 3227 cm−1 (Fig. S74c), explaining the negligible fluorescence from bulk P13 and confirming that the intermolecular hydrogen bonding indeed contribute to NTILs of aliphatic PAs. Compared to P9, due to PEG attachment (to preclude the crosslinking of PAs), the improved flexibility of P10 sacrificed much fluorescence and led to a lower Φ of 2.0%. Without PEGylation, the resultant PA (P14) could further promote the Φ to 8.8% (Fig. S83), but weak hydrogen bonding interaction through the system failed to enhance Φ more (Fig. S84). Collectively, different category of amides through aliphatic PAs possess distinct PIEs, and their photophysical properties can be readily regulated by altering the intermolecular forces among PA structures or with the environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Distinct PIE quenching effect by metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Since unconventional fluorescnece of aliphatic PAs result from different intermolecular (self or non-self) hydrogen bonding interactions, dual PIE emissions of aliphatic PAs should exhibit distinct fluorescence behavior upon complexation with metal ions. Generally, an amide can bind various metal ions via either oxygen or nitrogen atom [60]. In Singha’s two separate reports, two aliphatic polymers containing similar amide units exhibited different fluorence quneching effect upon various metal ions [13,61]. It is possbily ascribed to disturbance from additional functional groups and uncerntain polymer structures. Herein, we utilized PAs building from thiolactone chemistry to probe the influence of amide category on complexation with metal ions. As shown in Fig. 6 , incubation of P1 aqueous solution (1 mM) with different metal ions (10 mM Li+, K+, Cu+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Zr4+) for 1 h, P1 showed selective fluorescence quenching only by Fe3+ and Ag+ ions at both E m375 and E m440. Interestingly, K+, Mg2+ and Fe2+ ions only quenched the fluorescnce of P1 in E m375, but not in E m440. This difference probably results from the different metalation capabilities and hydrogen bonding interactions. The dissolution of high concentration of metal ions would compete with weak amide-solvent hydrogen bonding interaction to quench E m375, but strong amide-amide hydrogen bonding interaction would substantially retain E m440 to some extent. Meanwhile, different rotation barriers for secondary amides and tertiary amides would also deviate their fluorescence. Generally, the frequency of cis population in tertiary amides is much larger than that in secondary amides, and the rotation barriers of amide bonds increase as intercellular/intracellular hydrogen bonding interactions increase [29]. Therefore, it is anticipatied that the fluorescence reduction of E m375 would prevail over E m440, and PAs with less tertiary amides would retail more E m440 (Fig. 6G). Taking Ag+ ions as an example, we examined the fluorescence of P6 to prove our hypothesis. Fig. S85 shows that E m440 of P6 retained 42% of prestine fluorescnce, which was higher than that of P1 (29%). Consistent with previous results, these data again reveal that different amide categories contribute to distinct PIEs of fluorescent aliphatic PAs.

Inspired by that postive hetrocycles such as piperidine, piperazine and morpholine rings have been reported to be lysosome-targeting subunits [62–64], we expect P4 that was ring-opened by 1-methylpiperazine could selectively anchor to specific organelle due to its cationic structure. To facilitte the penetration into cells, an amphiphilic structure is preferred to self-assemble P4 into nanoparticles. Therefore, t-butyl methacrylate replaced PEG500 to consume in-situ generated thiols (Fig. 7 A). According to TEM and AFM analysis, P4 self-assembled into nanoparticles in aqueous solution and had a diameter of 21.6 nm and possessed a positive Zeta potential of 3.26 mV by DLS (Fig. 7B, 7C). The small size and positive charge should provide P4 nanoparticles with good penetration capability, and the protonation of piperazine groups could potentially locate in lysosome due to its low pH environment. On the HepG2 cell line, P4 presented excellent biocompatibility and did not show obvious cytotoxicity (Fig. 7D). The cell viability was still above 90% when the concentration of P4 reached to 2.5 mg mL−1. Afterwards, we investigated its cell imaging property by CLSM. As shown in Fig. 7E, P4 (1 × 10−5 M) quickly entered into the cytoplasm of HepG2 cells within 30 min. Costained with Lyso-Tracker Red, a commercial lysosome imaging agent, bright fluorescence of P4 from dot structures in cytoplasm was observed, which is the typical morphology of lysosome. The Pearson's correlation coefficient was 0.88, indicating P4 can specifically target lysosome. By contrast, when constained with MitoTracker Red CMXRos, a commercial mitochondrial imaging agent, the colocation correlation coefficient was 0.52, demonstrating that P4 cannot anchor mitochondrial. To emphasize the selectivity toward specific organelle, the incubation of P4 was strictly controlled within 60 min, since extending incubation time has been proved efficient to enter into cells and increase organelle accumulation [65].

In summary, thiolactone chemistry was employed to construct a library of fluorescent aliphatic PAs. Compared to traditional aliphatic PAs with NTILs at the current stage, aliphatic PAs derived from this platform were equipped with tunable amide category, controllable amide distribution, and variable chemical environment. These advantages inspire us to explore the structure-NTIL relationship of aliphatic PAs and decipher the divergent fluorescence of aliphatic PAs. Remarkably, aliphatic PAs have PIE property rather than AIE or AIEE, which is different from the previous works. Varied amides have different self or non-self-intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions and provide PAs with distinguishing emission centers, which exhibit distinct PIE quenching effect upon metal ions. These PIEs are truly λ ex-dependent, but not like multicolor emissions from an ensemble of fluorescent states. Moreover, the in-situ generated sulfhydryls could be utilized to dope and regulate the delicate structures of aliphatic polyamides by thiol-based click chemistry, and the resultant varying intermolecular forces among the system or with the environment devoted to adjusting the NTILs of aliphatic PAs. Thanks to CSAMA, the introduction of a piperazine provides the resultant PA with lysosome-specific targeting capability and lights up the organelle. Our study not only demonstrates how the category of amides and detailed sub-structures of amide chains, along with doped hetero atoms, can be realized by thiolactone chemistry and distinguish PIEs from varied aliphatic PAs, but also can expand the knowledge and applications of PIE and supplement the paradigm of NTIL.
